verses.text: The LORD: bring a red heifer without spot, on which no yoke ever came. Eleazar sprinkles her blood seven times before the tabernacle. Burn her — hide, flesh, blood, dung. Cast cedar, hyssop, scarlet into the fire. A clean man gathers the ashes outside the camp — for the water of separation, a purification for sin. Guidance: One spotless heifer outside the camp answers death itself. Her ashes wait in a clean place — pre-made medicine for the day someone touches a corpse. God prepared the cure before the wound.

He that toucheth a dead body is unclean seven days. He shall purify himself with the water on the third day and on the seventh day — and be clean. If he purify not, he is unclean still — that soul shall be cut off from Israel, for he hath defiled the tabernacle. Guidance: Death contaminates — but the cleansing is fixed, two appointments seven days apart. Skip them and the contamination stays. God provides the wash; the unwashed defile not just themselves but His house.

When a man dies in a tent, all that come into the tent are unclean seven days; every open vessel uncovered is unclean. Whosoever toucheth in the field one slain, a dead body, a bone, or a grave — unclean seven days. Guidance: Death spreads. It seeps into the room, the dishes left uncovered, the bone in the field. The chapter does not flinch at how thorough mortality is. But the next paragraph has the answer — the ashes were ready before the body fell.

For the unclean: take ashes of the burnt heifer, running water in a vessel. A clean person dip hyssop, sprinkle on the tent, the vessels, the persons. On the third day and the seventh, the unclean shall be purified; wash clothes, bathe — clean at evening. Guidance: Ashes plus running water plus hyssop — the kit God designed for death. The clean person carries the cleansing TO the unclean; the unclean cannot reach for it himself. Grace is delivered, not earned.

The unclean man who purifies not shall be cut off, for he hath defiled the sanctuary. The water of separation was not sprinkled. He that sprinkleth shall wash his clothes; he that toucheth shall be unclean till even. Guidance: Even the one administering cleansing becomes unclean — the holiness of the act consumes its server. The priest pays a small price to bring purity. A foreshadow of the One who bore the whole uncleanness on Himself so we could come home clean.
